examples.title: Telnet Connection Establishment
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how a Telnet client connects to a remote server
examples.code:
TELNET_CLIENT_CONNECT(host, port)
BEGIN
 IF port = 23 THEN
 socket ← CREATE_SOCKET()
 CONNECT(socket, host, port)
 IF connection_successful THEN
 PRINT "Connected to " + host
 SET escape_character TO Ctrl+]
 WHILE session_active DO
 input ← READ_USER_INPUT()
 IF input = escape_character THEN
 ENTER_COMMAND_MODE()
 ELSE
 nvt_data ← CONVERT_TO_NVT(input)
 SEND(socket, nvt_data)
 END IF
 END WHILE
 ELSE
 PRINT "Connection refused"
 END IF
 ELSE
 PRINT "Invalid port for Telnet"
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the basic Telnet client connection process: creating a socket, connecting to port 23, handling user input, converting to NVT format, and managing the escape character for command mode.

examples.title: Telnet Option Negotiation
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating Telnet option negotiation using WILL/WONT/DO/DONT
examples.code:
TELNET_OPTION_NEGOTIATION(socket, option)
BEGIN
 // Option negotiation commands
 IAC ← 255 // Interpret As Command
 WILL ← 251
 WONT ← 252
 DO ← 253
 DONT ← 254
 
 // Example: Client requesting server to enable ECHO
 IF client_requesting_echo THEN
 SEND(socket, [IAC, DO, ECHO])
 response ← RECEIVE(socket)
 IF response = [IAC, WILL, ECHO] THEN
 enable_local_echo()
 ELSE
 use_remote_echo()
 END IF
 END IF
 
 // Server handling WILL request
 IF server_receives_will_echo THEN
 IF server_supports_echo THEN
 SEND(socket, [IAC, DO, ECHO])
 enable_remote_echo()
 ELSE
 SEND(socket, [IAC, DONT, ECHO])
 END IF
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: This shows how Telnet option negotiation works using the WILL/WONT/DO/DONT commands. The client requests an option (DO), and the server responds with willingness (WILL) or refusal (WONT).
